title: When One Says Tiruchi, Is It Not a Turning-Point Conference? (3)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. This translation preserves the printed conference chronology, historical speeches, quotations and poem. The poetic passage was visually resolved against the source pages; the canonical source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: Between the birth of the DMK on 17 September 1949 and its first State Conference in Chennai beginning on 13 December 1951, twelve party conferences were held within just two years. Even among district conferences, the DMK's very first was the Tiruchi district conference, held in this same Tiruchi on 1 January 1950. Thus Tiruchi has the distinction of having conducted the party's first conference when the DMK was a three-month-old infant still crawling. Now that the party has completed 64 years, Tiruchi also receives the radiant distinction of hosting the tenth State Conference. At that first Tiruchi conference, Perarignar Anna's play *Chandramohan* was staged, with Anna himself acting in it. That is history.

When Anna took the lead in conducting the first State Conference in Chennai in 1951, the enormous task of raising money for its expenses fell upon him and us. Each day, while Anna sat with comrades in Chennai and personally supervised work on the pavilion, C.V.M. Annamalai took me to meetings across Chengalpattu district and collected Rs. 200 for the conference fund at each one. There would be seven or eight meetings a day, yielding a total of Rs. 1,600 or Rs. 1,800. He would serve our night meal only at two or three in the morning. After completing every meeting, C.V.M. and I would arrive directly at the SIAA Grounds with one or two thousand rupees.

Anna would be sitting on the sand piled for the conference, with comrades around him. Navalar, Perasiriyar, E.V.K. Sampath and Nanjilar would all be talking there. We would proudly hand Anna the money. He would take it and ask, “Where did you eat?” “At Uthiramerur, Anna,” I would answer. Before I could reply to his next question, “What did you eat?”, Anna himself would joke, “Would not Annamalai have told them to prepare everything he wanted in your name?”

After conducting its first conference in Tiruchi in January 1950, the DMK held Chennai district's first conference on 21 January and Coimbatore district's on the 29th. A DMK Women's Conference was held in Chennai on 4 April 1950 under Moovalur elder Ramamirtham Ammaiyar. Madurai district's first conference followed on 10 June; Tirunelveli district's on 26 and 27 August; the Chennai district Dravida Students Progressive Federation conference on 8 November; Chengai district's on 23 and 24 December; Kumari district's on 29 and 30 December; Thanjavur district's on 3 and 4 March 1951; Salem district's on 5 and 6 May; and a Conference for Freedom of Writing and Speech at Dindigul on 29 July. Only after these was the first State General Conference held in Chennai that December.

Anna assigned me to preside over the first Tirunelveli district conference on 26 and 27 August 1950. Its inaugurator was philosopher T.K. Srinivasan, the distinguished father of today's organising secretary Thambi T.K.S. Elangovan MP, whose amplified voice heard from a distance sounded remarkably like Anna's. It was the first conference over which I presided. I was only 26.

In my presidential address I said: “The purpose of giving this distinction to people like me is to demonstrate to the country, and to those who circulate poisonous ideas about us, that the movement belongs to the young, that it is a mansion built by workers, and that pleasure-seekers cannot live within it. The country looks to us in expectation and trust. It sharpens its ears and attends to every word we speak. We have become one of the largest parties in the Indian subcontinent. We are inferior to no other party. In the South, the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam receives greater welcome than the regard enjoyed by Congress, the opinion held of the Socialists, the support shown to the Hindu Mahasabha, or the concern shown for the Communists!

“Indeed, ours is the only Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam to possess the South's finest orators and architects of a new world who scatter thought in abundance, and to labour ceaselessly with devotion to duty. We have grown, and we continue to grow.

“We fight for justice and integrity. We accept hardship with a smile and perform public service. Our principles cure the nation's decline and bring well-being. Ours did not dissolve when struck by stones and words. It did not die when the spears of wealthy influence were hurled. It did not perish under the opposition of pontiffs or the fury of mahants and jeeyars. It escaped the bonds of those who desired only office and titles, and the demonic grip of those who valued only private life and selfish gain.

“It is the movement that embraced Nagai Mani, Mayavaram Natarajan, Arur V.S. Raman and Alagirisamy, who lived for principle; that possessed Thalamuthu and Natarajan, who gave their lives for language; that produces Udaiyarpalayam Velayudhams, hanged lifeless from trees for their principles; that did not lose heart before the fire at the Madurai conference or tremble at the flood of blood in the streets of Kudanthai; that demonstrated the defects of the Ramayana and defeated Bharathiar and Sethu Pillai in verbal battle; that possesses thousands of Thambis who sing, ‘Death at six or death at a hundred, let whatever happens happen’; that possesses Anna, one of the world's scholars, who taps his brothers' hearts, asks whether their resolve is firm, and awaits the proper time for a moral struggle; and that possesses Puducherry poet Bharathidasan, the doom that tears open the entrails of the jackal pack, the rumbling volcano. From then until now, we have borne successive dangers, crossed beyond them and grown.

“In these eleven months there are more than five hundred branches, six district conferences, and many thousands who listen daily to our principles with concern. People ask which other party could have achieved this. We ourselves rejoice in our growth.”
I spoke thus about the movement's development in my presidential address at the Kovilpatti conference. It was a long speech, and there is no room to reproduce it fully here.

As conference president, I also said that the party's organisation must spread through village, town, circle and district branches to headquarters; that every body must remain connected; and that a chain of communication must carry headquarters directives immediately down to village branches. I pointed to Tamil Nadu's neglect and urged the party to devise struggles against Hindi domination. In his concluding speech, Anna accepted all the deficiencies I had identified and announced protest plans at the conference as I had requested. Perarignar Anna published the address I delivered at 26 on the front page of his *Dravida Nadu*, accompanied by my photograph. That published photograph is enclosed with this letter.

At the second State Conference in Tiruchi, I spoke on “Dravida's International Connections.” Even then, DMK conferences assigned subjects in this manner. I began: “Dravidians suffer in Malayan rubber plantations, Ceylonese tea estates and African mines. They are treated like animals, live beside drains and labour like cattle. In those countries they are reduced to degrading conditions, deprived of rights, and shed tears. This is Dravida's international connection today.”

I further said: “Yesterday we lived; today we have fallen; tomorrow we shall live, and for that we shall take up the sword! We shall pledge ourselves! For that very purpose, we have assembled at this State Conference here in Tiruchi, land where warriors roam.” The venue now is the same Tiruchi. Does not what I said in 1956 appear applicable even today? That is why I recall it.
Thambi Kavithaipithan has garlanded the forthcoming tenth State Conference in Tiruchi with these richly expressive words:
“In the conference history of the party that fills the heart, a conference that will endure as an inscription of memories!
A resurgent conference that kindles the fire of ethnic feeling in folds of the mind grown blunt!

Leveling the sand mounds that lay like forest, overgrown with thorn and cactus, turning them into a brand-new town, against the majestic backdrop of ‘Irattai Malai,’ rising to rival the hills and shimmering with beauty!

In Tamil Nadu, surrounded by adversity like a hall filled with darkness, transforming the state of gathering delusion and dispelling confusion, kindling the sun's light beside Tiruchi's ‘Karumandapam,’ a conference that will win glory for Pirattiyur!”

Speaking at the second State Conference in this same Tiruchi, Anna said: “You who have assembled here have come in hundreds of thousands from Valparai at an elevation of 4,000 feet, from the distant harbour town of Thoothukudi, from Chennai considered the capital, and, most especially, from the villages that must be singled out for distinction. Looking at the country and its rulers, I wish to remind them with a little pride: ‘One lakh people attended the State Conference we convened in Chennai. If you wish to see the growth we have now attained, we shall gather three lakh people at the State Conference in Tiruchi.’

“After saying it, even I wondered whether I had exaggerated a little. Today, wherever we look, we see DMK comrades. Not only across this vast conference ground, but everywhere for six or seven miles from the grounds to Tiruchi Junction, along all the highways, we see our comrades filling the space.”

When our leader Anna had already declared at the second State Conference that we would gather three lakh people, is this not now the tenth? Should we not assemble at least ten lakh? After district secretaries conduct their meetings, reports in *Murasoli* say, “So many thousands will participate from our district.” Every wing says likewise. Add those figures together. Will you not gather ten lakh?

Reception committee chairman Thambi K.N. Nehru, working energetically on the pavilion, must surely take the precaution: “The Leader has said ten lakh people; should we not build a pavilion for them?” That is why I cited Anna's words. Our treasurer Thambi Stalin inspected the site while work was underway so that corrections could be made in time. He told reporters: “The pavilion will be 1,100 feet long and 600 feet wide. The stage will measure 200 by 80 feet. A palm-leaf facade will be constructed. In keeping with his age, the flagstaff will be 90 feet high. One hundred acres are being prepared for comrades' vehicles. The total conference area is 200 acres. Three major access roads and more than one hundred wedding halls for accommodation are being arranged. At least five lakh people can be within the pavilion and another five lakh around it, so arrangements have been made for ten lakh people in all to attend this victory conference.”

After he himself has said this, can I reduce the figure? There is no doubt that ten lakh people will gather at the Tiruchi conference. Should you not be one among them? Is it not my duty to remind you to prepare?
Today is 12 January. The conference is on 15 February. Only one month remains!
Affectionately,
M.K.
12 January 2014
